Obviously certain basic backgroundchecks are logical and should be done if any real security info is going to be passedonto outsiders; however, these are a couple of levels below real security clearancesand can stay that way. Trusting one’s own judgment is much more important. TheU.S. can learn much about this from some European countries, especially the UK.It is not an exaggeration to claim that an independent, vibrant, and engaged civil societyis one of the unique indicators of a liberal democracy. The fact that they are a benefit,not a cost, is most evident in security trust networks. Democratic governments woulddo well to support them as a centerpiece in Whole of Nation cyber security.Alexander Klimburg is a Fellow at the Austrian Institute for International Affairs. Sincejoining the Institute in October 2006, he has worked on a number of government nationalsecurity research projects. Alexander has partaken in international and inter-govern-mental discussions, and he acts as a scientific advisor on cyber security to the Austriandelegation to the OSCE as well as other bodies. He is regularly consulted by national andinternational media as well as private businesses.202|Chapter 13: Advice for Policy Makers from the Field

AfterwordIn Chapter 1, I presented a future threat scenario that involved a catastrophic chainreaction resulting from a coordinated hacker attack against multiple nuclear powerplants. Granted, I painted a worst-case scenario. Since writing that chapter, I’vechanged my thinking on what a worst-case scenario really is, as recent real-life eventshave shown that threats to critical infrastructure are becoming increasingly frequent.On January 18, 2008, senior CIA analyst Tom Donahue told attendees at a SANS con-ference that a cyber attack against a foreign nation’s electric grid resulted in a wide-spread power outage that affected multiple cities. He did not disclose the name of thecountry or the date of the event. The unique nature of Donahue’s disclosure (the CIAalmost never does this) and the mystery surrounding the actual event attracted theproducers of CBS News’ 60 Minutes, and in the fall of 2009, they launched their owninvestigation into the situation. The findings aired on November 8, 2009 (see the seg-ment at http://www.cbsnews.com/stories/2009/11/06/60minutes/main5555565.shtml),wherein they named the country that Donahue was referring to in his presentation atthe SANS conference—Brazil:Several prominent intelligence sources confirmed that there were a series of cyber attacksin Brazil: one north of Rio de Janeiro in January 2005 that affected three cities and tensof thousands of people, and another, much larger event beginning on Sept. 26, 2007.That one in the state of Espirito Santo affected more than three million people in dozensof cities over a two-day period, causing major disruptions. In Vitoria, the world’s largestiron ore producer had seven plants knocked offline, costing the company $7 million. Itis not clear who did it or what the motive was.On the Monday after the 60 Minutes aired, Brazilian government officials, as well asthe utility company that supplies power to the affected region, immediately disputedthe report, claiming “sooty insulators” were the problem, not hackers. Within 24 hoursof that public announcement, Brazil suffered another power outage affecting 90 millionpeople in Rio, Sao Paulo, and Paraguay. This remarkable act of coincidence, karma,bad luck, poor infrastructure, or an angry hacker crew is where the story gets interest-ing. Because of the exposure 60 Minutes provided, more people were paying attention,and when the expected denial of responsibility came from ONS (Brazil’s national power203

grid operator) and some officials of the Brazilian government (they blamed it on alightning strike hitting power lines), the public didn’t accept it at face value.Within hours, a Brazilian hacker discovered a vulnerability of the ONS website andwrote about it on his blog (http://blog.hacknroll.com/2009/11/12/a-verdade-sobre-o-apagao/), while technicians at Brazil’s Group of Atmospheric Electricity INPE saidthere was no lightning in the area at the time of the outage.In other words, an informed public does make a difference, and that’s the entire pointand my hope for this book: the facts, opinions, and analysis presented within thesepages will hopefully result in more attention being paid to the many known vulnera-bilities that remain unpatched in our high-value networks, including critical infra-structure. In some cases, the problem is due to political pressure or conflictingpriorities. In other cases, it’s a simple matter of greed; i.e., the cost of securing the powergrid would hurt profits and that, in the minds of certain asset owners and operators, isreason enough not to do it.In the United States, about 90% of the power grid is privately owned and self-regulating(SRO) through the North American Electric Reliability Corporation (NERC). NERC’sSRO status means that it has the final word on whether it will comply with federal cybersecurity standards. As I write this, the House Committee on Energy is holding hearingson a bill (HR 2165) to give the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) morepower over NERC, so as to force the issue of cyber security–compliance. NERC isheavily contesting the bill, even as its outgoing CEO, Rick Sergel, publicly recognizedthat “questions will be coming” (http://www.nerc.com/fileUploads/File/News/Executive-Remarks.110509.pdf) and that if NERC doesn’t act soon, it may lose its ability toself-regulate.Unfortunately, one of the areas that I didn’t cover in this book is the extent of theproblem that we face because of inaction on the part of NERC and its member com-panies. I only became aware of the extent of the problem in late August, about the timethat this manuscript was due. Since then, Project Grey Goose has announced a fullopen source investigation into hacker attacks against U.S. and international electricgrids, and the extent of the problem is stunning. Many NERC members are resistantto even identifying their critical assets (29% compliance among generation owners and63% compliance among transmission owners [http://clarke.house.gov/2009/10/house-hearing-highlights-fight-over-ferc-cybersecurity-powers-energy-washington-week.shtml]), let alone repairing known vulnerabilities.Information is even withheld from U.S. National Labs such as INL that produced theOctober 2005 report “Cyber Incidents Involving Control Systems,” wherein the authorswrote “Most companies prefer not to share cyber attack incident data because of po-tential financial repercussions. Uniform reporting requirements will do much to makethis information available to Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and others whorequire it.” In spite of the uncooperative nature of many of the private companies that204|Afterword

control the grid, this report still managed to document 120 cyber security incidents(while not revealing any identifying information).Finally, an October 2009 GAO report, “Defense Critical Infrastructure: Actions Nee-ded to Improve the Identification and Management of Electrical Power Risks and Vul-nerabilities to DOD Critical Assets,” revealed that 31 out of 34 of the DOD’s mostcritical assets are dependent upon the public power grid, which is 90% privately ownedand, under current law, cannot be ordered to comply with hardening its networksagainst cyber attacks—absolutely astounding in its national security implications.Why talk about the power grid now? It’s been in the background for much of this book.The problems facing the power grid are paradigmatic for every level of our infrastruc-ture, even things that you don’t normally associate with computing, like the highways.(A few years ago, hackers attacked the remotely controlled highway signs in Connect-icut. It was just a prank, but it didn’t have to be a prank.) The biggest problem we’refacing isn’t the attack itself, it’s the head-in-the-sand attitude that prevents organiza-tions from putting the security practices and safeguards in place that would make anattack less likely to succeed, and less harmful if it did succeed. And what drives thisattitude is often the legal system; an admission of vulnerability is often an admissionof liability. Unfortunately, the best way to ensure that an attack succeeds is to denythat the attack can happen.If this book has accomplished anything, it’s shown that cyber warfare is increasinglycommon, vulnerabilities exist at every level of our infrastructure, and the worst possibleresponse is to deny that the problem exists. Whether the attackers are military profes-sionals, cyber criminals, or political and religious zealots, they aren’t going away. Atthe beginning of this afterword, I said that my opinion on the worst-case future threatscenario had changed: it’s not a hacker triggering a nuclear catastrophe or amultimillion-node botnet freezing an entire nation’s networks. A true nightmare wouldbe if, one year from today, nothing has changed.Afterword|205

About the AuthorJeffrey Carr is a cyber intelligence expert, consultant, and author who specializes inthe investigation of cyber attacks against governments and infrastructures by state andnon-state hackers.Jeffrey is the principal investigator for Project Grey Goose, an open source intelligenceinvestigation into the Russian cyber attacks on Georgia in August 2008. The GreyGoose Phase I and Phase II reports have been widely read and well received throughoutthe intelligence, defense, and law enforcement agencies of Western governments.His work has been quoted in the New York Times, the Washington Post, the Guardian,BusinessWeek, Parameters, and Wired.ColophonThe image on the cover of Inside Cyber Warfare is of light cavalry, as evidenced by thelack of armor adorning the soldier and his horse. During Roman-Germanic wars, theduties of reconnaissance, screening, and raiding fell on the light cavalry, while theirmore heavily armored counterparts engaged in direct enemy combat. Their weaponsincluded spears, bows, and swords.The tribes of Central Asia, including the Huns, Turks, and Mongols, often used lightcavalry for similar missions.It is important to note that practices, weapons, and so on varied depending upon his-torical period and region.The cover image is from the Dover Pictorial Archive. The cover font is Adobe ITCGaramond. The text font is Linotype Birka; the heading font is Adobe Myriad Con-densed; and the code font is LucasFont’s TheSansMonoCondensed.
